fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a background task orchestration thing for the datenlord project. Basically the problem is that when we try to shut down the service, things go haywire — some workers are stopping before the things they depend on, which causes panics and data loss. It's been a recurring ops headache and a few customers have complained about unclean shutdowns corrupting state.

The idea is to build a library where we declare the relationships between different parts of the system once, and then when we get a kill signal or manually trigger a stop, everything winds down in the right order automatically. Kind of like how we handled the dependency ordering in that older registry module — same concept but generalized.

There are a few tricky bits: some nodes handle tons of tiny short-lived tasks and we don't want to register each one individually, so there should be some kind of batching mode for those. Also we had a bug last quarter where work was still being submitted after teardown started and silently getting dropped — that needs to be a proper error instead. And obviously OS signals (the usual termination one) should trigger the same clean shutdown path.

The whole thing should be split into sensible modules, not one giant file. The test harness should run from rcb_tests/test.sh and output files per case into a namespaced stdout folder. Let me know if anything is unclear, I can get the tech lead to clarify the node names.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func RunAdapter(r io.Reader, w io.Writer) error

trigger_keywords: what are the node names
which nodes exist in the graph
what are the valid node types
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fixed set of node names is: Root, Metrics, BlockFlush, FuseRequest, AsyncFuse, Rpc, WriteBack, SchedulerExtender. These are the only valid node identifiers in the dependency graph.

trigger_keywords: what are the dependencies between nodes
which node depends on which
what is the dependency graph structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dependency edges are: Root depends on Metrics, BlockFlush, and SchedulerExtender. BlockFlush depends on AsyncFuse and FuseRequest. FuseRequest depends on AsyncFuse and WriteBack. AsyncFuse depends on Rpc and WriteBack. All other nodes (Metrics, Rpc, WriteBack, SchedulerExtender) have no dependencies.

trigger_keywords: what is the shutdown order
in what sequence are nodes torn down
which nodes shut down first
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The shutdown order is: Metrics, BlockFlush, SchedulerExtender (first tier, all with marker 0 in the full example), then FuseRequest (marker 1), then AsyncFuse (marker 2), then Rpc and WriteBack (both marker 3). A node is only torn down after every node that depends on it has already been torn down. When only a subset of nodes is registered, only those nodes appear in output, preserving the same relative order.

trigger_keywords: which nodes support batching
what are the garbage-collected nodes
which nodes have batch or gc mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only BlockFlush and FuseRequest are garbage-collected (GC) nodes. They expose a lightweight batching handle that funnels many short-lived tasks into one background collector. Other nodes do not support batching.

trigger_keywords: what should happen when spawning after shutdown
how is a post-shutdown registration error reported
what is the error format for late registration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a worker registration is attempted after shutdown has begun, the output must be exactly two lines: first 'error=spawn_after_shutdown', then 'node=<NodeName>' echoing the rejected node. No exception message or runtime detail should appear. This is a domain-level normalized error, not a panic or silent drop.

trigger_keywords: how does signal shutdown work
what is the output format for signal-triggered shutdown
what signal is supported for termination
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For signal-triggered shutdown, the output begins with 'signal=<label>' (e.g., 'signal=SIGTERM') on its own line, followed by the standard dependency-ordered node teardown lines in the same format as explicit shutdown. The signal line always precedes all node lines. SIGTERM is the termination signal used in tests.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for each worker
how is a node teardown line formatted
what does each worker emit on shutdown
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each worker emits exactly one line in the format: 'node=<NodeName> marker=<marker>' (e.g., 'node=Metrics marker=0'). For batched GC nodes, one such line is emitted per enqueued task, so a node with count=5 produces 5 identical lines.

trigger_keywords: how should the test harness work
how does test.sh output files
where are test output files written
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked as 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' and accepts '--cases-dir <subdir>' (default: test_cases). For each case in each *.json file it writes one file to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt (e.g., rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_ordered_shutdown@000.txt). Each file contains only raw stdout from the program. Output is namespaced by cases-dir so different runs do not overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be structured
what modules should exist
how should concerns be separated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must use a multi-module layout separating: (1) the dependency-graph model, (2) the node/worker registry, (3) the shutdown driver/sequencer, (4) the batching/GC collector, and (5) the signal hook. The execution/IO adapter that reads JSON from stdin and writes to stdout must be physically and logically separate from the core orchestration logic. A single god-file is not acceptable.

trigger_keywords: what happens with batching drain output order
are gc batch tasks interleaved between nodes
how are multiple gc nodes drained
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All tasks belonging to one garbage-collected node are drained together (producing consecutive lines for that node) before moving on to the next GC node. Tasks from different GC nodes are never interleaved. The GC nodes themselves still respect the overall dependency-ordered shutdown sequence (BlockFlush before FuseRequest in the defined graph order).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'ordered_shutdown', 'signal_shutdown', 'spawn_after_shutdown', 'gc_batch_drain'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: kind of like how we handled the dependency ordering in that older registry module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fixed dependency graph defined in the graph model module, specifically the edges: Root→{Metrics,BlockFlush,SchedulerExtender}, BlockFlush→{AsyncFuse,FuseRequest}, FuseRequest→{AsyncFuse,WriteBack}, AsyncFuse→{Rpc,WriteBack}, used for topological sort during shutdown sequencing
SIGTERM (syscall.SIGTERM), which is the OS termination signal that triggers the signal_shutdown path, reported as 'signal=SIGTERM' in output

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to check with the tech lead or look at the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact contract. The test files are the source of truth for expected output format.

feature1_ordered_shutdown.json.description: Background workers are registered against named nodes of a fixed dependency graph, each carrying an integer marker. When a graceful shutdown is requested, every worker is cancelled and reports its marker. The reports are emitted strictly in the graph's shutdown order: a node is only torn down after every node that depends on it has already been torn down, so upstream producers stop before the consumers they feed.

cases.expected_output:
node=Metrics marker=0
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=SchedulerExtender marker=0
node=FuseRequest marker=1
node=AsyncFuse marker=2
node=Rpc marker=3
node=WriteBack marker=3

node=BlockFlush marker=10
node=AsyncFuse marker=20
node=Rpc marker=30

feature2_signal_shutdown.json.description: Instead of an explicit shutdown call, a process-level termination signal initiates the graceful shutdown. After registering workers and arming the signal handler, a termination signal is raised. The handler reports the signal it observed and then drives the exact same dependency-ordered teardown, so every worker reports its marker in graph order following the signal line.

cases.expected_output:
signal=SIGTERM
node=Metrics marker=0
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=SchedulerExtender marker=0
node=FuseRequest marker=1
node=AsyncFuse marker=2
node=Rpc marker=3
node=WriteBack marker=3

feature3_spawn_after_shutdown.json.description: Once a graceful shutdown has been initiated, the registry refuses to accept any further work. Attempting to register a new worker on any node after shutdown is rejected, and the rejection reports the node that was refused rather than silently succeeding.

feature4_gc_batch_drain.json.description: Certain nodes are garbage-collected nodes: instead of holding workers directly, they hand out a lightweight handle that batches many short-lived tasks into a single background collector. Multiple tasks are enqueued per garbage-collected node; on shutdown the collector drains every enqueued task and each reports its marker. All tasks belonging to one garbage-collected node are drained together before moving to the next node.

cases.expected_output:
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=BlockFlush marker=0
node=FuseRequest marker=1
node=FuseRequest marker=1
node=FuseRequest marker=1
node=FuseRequest marker=1
node=FuseRequest marker=1
